name: Krasue
alias: Ahp · Kasu · the floating head that hunts in the dark
body: First there is a glow low over the paddy, soft and red like a lantern carried by no one, drifting against the wind. Then the smell finds you, a wet reek of blood and rot that no village should carry on a clean night. By day the woman next door is unremarkable, even beautiful, polite at the market and slow to anger. It is only after dark, when the rice fields breathe out their mist, that you might see what travels above them: a human head, lovely and lit from within, dragging beneath it the slick rope of its own organs, the heart and stomach and lungs still glistening as they sway.

The Krasue hunts what is soft and unguarded. She craves blood, the leavings of birth, raw flesh, and filth, and so she haunts the places where these are found, the byre, the latrine, and above all the bed of a labouring woman. To recognise her is to understand the bargain at her heart: by night the head detaches and flies free to feed, while the body it abandoned lies hidden and helpless, and the head must return and rejoin it before dawn or be destroyed. Some say the curse is inherited, passed mother to daughter; some say it is the punishment of a sin in a former life, a debt the body pays nightly in its own glowing entrails.

What lingers is the cruelty of the everyday face, the certainty that the smiling neighbour may unspool at midnight into something hunting your child. Farmers in the northeast plant sharp thorned branches around the house and beneath the floor of a birthing room, for entrails caught and torn on thorns cannot heal before sunrise. In the villages of Isan they still tell of a head found tangled at first light in the bamboo, mouth open over a stolen meal, the body it belonged to never breathing again.
note: Wards are practical and old: thorny branches (often bamboo or the thorns of citrus and sang khee) ringed around the house and under the birthing floor to snare the trailing organs, and the certain kill, finding the abandoned body by daylight and hiding, moving, or destroying it so the head cannot reattach before dawn. The Krasue is the Thai member of a wider Southeast Asian family of detaching female revenants: the Malay and Indonesian Penanggalan, the Cambodian Ahp (Krasue), the Lao Kasu or Phi Kasu, the Balinese Leyak, and the Philippine Manananggal. Across the region the shared logic holds: a woman by day, a flying viscera-trailing head by night, vulnerable in the moment of return to her own flesh.

The tale is told across Thailand and especially throughout Isan (the northeast), as well as in Laos and Cambodia, and remains a staple of regional horror cinema. Folk explanations range from inherited curse to karmic punishment for sorcery or for eating taboo food while alive.
